elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate a class of combinatorial sums involving reciprocals of central binomial coefficients , employing generating functions as the primary solution technique to formulate and analyze series involving the Catalan's constant.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using a direct approach, we derive new identities through integral techniques.
elements.sent-2411-003: Keywords: Catalan's constant, central binomial coefficients, reciprocals of binomial coefficients.. Hyper-geometric functions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, it is defined as the alternating sum of the reciprocals of the odd square numbers.
elements.sent-2411-005: This can be mathematically expressed as follows: \begin{equation}G:=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{(2n+1)^2}\approx 0.915965..\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-006: This intriguing constant arises in various mathematical contexts, particularly in number theory and combinatorial mathematics, where it serves as a fundamental element in the study of series and special functions.
elements.sent-2411-007: The behavior of Catalan's constant reveals deep connections to other mathematical constants and functions, showcasing the rich interplay within the realm of mathematical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-008: In (\cite{David ,Pg 2), we have that, \begin{equation}G=\int_0^1\frac{\arctan x}{x}\,dx=\frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{x}{\cosh x}\,dx=2\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\log(2\cos x)\,dx\end{equation} which serves as some basic integral representation of $G$.\par Now, the binomial coefficient $\binom{n {m}$ is defined by \begin{equation}\binom{n}{m}= \frac{n!}{m!(n-m)!}, if \ n\geq m; 0, if \ n<m. \end{equation} where $n$ and $m$ are non-negative integers.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: For some results involving the inverse of binomial coefficients, see \cite{Batir ,\cite{Batirr ,\cite{Lehmer .\par Among the conclusions drawn in this paper, we will ascertain that if $n$ is a non-negative integer then \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\binom{2n+2}{n+1}\frac{1}{16^n(n+1)(2n+3)}=\frac{22}{3}-\frac{8}{\pi}-\frac{16G}{\pi}\end{equation} Throughout this paper, we verify our results using Computer Algebra System (CAS) software Mathematica 13.3.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Notice that we can write as follows; \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{x^n}{n^2\binom{2n}{n}} =\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\int_0^1\frac{x^n(w-w^2)^{n-1}}{n}\,dw=\frac{1}{2}\int_0^1\frac{1}{w(1-w)}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(wx(1-w))^2}{n}\,dw\end{equation} Note that $-\log(1-x)=\sum_{n=1 ^{\infty}\frac{x^n {n}$, thus we have \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2}\int_0^1\frac{1}{w(1-w)}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(wx(1-w))^2}{n}\,dw=-\frac{1}{2}\underbrace{\int_0^1\frac{\log(xw^2-xw+1)}{w(1-w)}\,dw}_{J(x)}\end{equation} Observe, \begin{equation}J'(x)=2\int_0^1\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\frac{\log(xw^2-xw+1)}{w}\,dw=-\frac{4}{x}\sqrt{\frac{x}{4-x}}\arctan\sqrt{\frac{x}{4-x}}\end{equation} Integrating both sides, we obtain the desired result.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: From Lemma \ref{lemma 1 , multiply both sides by $\sqrt{x}$ and then integrate with respect to $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: It can be shown that \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\frac{x^{2n}}{4^n}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-1\end{equation} Thus, multiply both sides of the above identity by $x^2$ and then integrate both sides to get, \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\frac{x^{2n+3}}{(2n+3)4^n}=\frac{1}{6}(3\arcsin x-3x\sqrt{1-x^2}-2x^3)+C\end{equation} Observe, as $x\to 0$ we have that $C\to 0$.. Hence $C=0$.
elements.sent-2411-021: By dividing through by $x^2$ and integrating both sides, we obtain; \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\frac{x^{2n+2}}{2^{2n+1}(n+1)(2n+3)}=-\frac{x^2}{6}-\frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{2}-\frac{\arcsin x}{2x}+C_1\end{equation} Similarly as $x\to 0$, we have that $C_1\to 1$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Since, we can show that \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\frac{x^{2n+1}}{4^n(2n+1)}=\arcsin x-x\end{equation} Integrating both sides of the above identity we get a new identity \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\frac{x^{2n+2}}{4^n(2n+1)(2n+2)}=x\arcsin x+\sqrt{1-x^2}-\frac{x^2}{2}-1\end{equation} Dividing both sides by $x$ and integrating both sides.
elements.sent-2411-027: Notice, \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\frac{x^{2n+2}}{2^{2n+1}(n+1)(2n+1)}=x\arcsin x+\sqrt{1-x^2}-\frac{x^2}{2}-1\end{equation} The result follows from the above identity.
elements.sent-2411-028: If $n$ is a non-negative integer, then we have \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\binom{2n+2}{n+1}\frac{1}{16^n(n+1)(2n+3)}=\frac{22}{3}-\frac{8}{\pi}-\frac{16G}{\pi}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-029: From Lemma \ref{lemma 4 , set $x=\sin y$ and using the walli's integral formula; \begin{equation}\binom{2n}{n}=\frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}2^{2n}\sin^{2n}t\,dt\end{equation} Thus, \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\binom{2n+2}{n+1}\frac{\pi}{2^{4n+4}(n+1)(2n+3)} =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(1-\frac{\sin^2x}{6}-\frac{\cos x}{2}-\frac{x}{2\sin x}\right)\,dx =\frac{11\pi}{24}-\frac{1}{2}-G\end{equation} Since $\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x {2\sin x}\,\mathrm{d x=G$, check [\cite{David ,Pg 2].}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: If n is a non-negative integer, then \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\binom{2n+2}{n+1}\frac{1}{64^n(n+1)^2(2n+1)}=8\log 2-\frac{17}{2}+\frac{24}{\pi}-\frac{16 G}{\pi}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: From Lemma \ref{lemma 5 , Set $x=\sin y$, while integrating from $0$ to $\pi/2$ and using the identity; \begin{equation}\label{7} \frac{\pi}{2^{2n+3}}\binom{2n+2}{n+1}=\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2n+2}t\,\mathrm{d}t\end{equation} Note that the above equality follows directly from the walli's integral formula.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The proof is straightforward from this end.
elements.sent-2411-034: If $n$ is a non-negative integer, then \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\binom{2n}{n}\binom{2n+2}{n+1}\frac{1}{16^n(n+1)(2n+1)(2n+3)}=\frac{20}{\pi}+\frac{8G}{\pi}-\frac{26}{3}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: From Lemma \ref{lemma 6 , following same pattern in Theorem 2 and using \eqref{7 with the famous identity from [\cite{David ,Pg 2]; \begin{equation}\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{2\sin x}\,dx=G\end{equation} The desired result follows immediately.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: From Lemma \ref{lemma 1 to Lemma \ref{lemma 3 , we can generate some Lehmer Series [check, \cite{Lehmer ] \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{2^n}{n}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1}=\frac{\pi}{2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{4^n}{n^2}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1}=\frac{\pi^2}{2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^2(n+2)}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1}=\frac{1}{72}(-117+42\pi\sqrt{3}-10\pi^2) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n(n+3/2)}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1}=\frac{100}{9}-2\sqrt{3}\pi \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n(n+3/2)(n+5/2)}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1}=\frac{4}{225}(-1384+255\pi\sqrt{3})\end{equation} We also have, \begin{equation}\label{12} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n^2}{16^n(2n-1)^2(2n+1)}\binom{2n}{n}^2 = \frac{G}{4\pi}+\frac{1}{8\pi}\end{equation} It's easy to derive \eqref{12 from \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{2n^2x^{2n-1}}{4^n(2n-1)^2(2n+1)}\binom{2n}{n}=\frac{1}{8x^2}\left((2x^2+1)\arcsin x-x\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)\end{equation} Now multiply by $x$ and set $\sin t=x$, then we integrate both sides from 0 to $\pi/2$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Observe, \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n(n+3/2)}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1} =\frac{1}{105} \ _2F_1\left[2,2;\frac{9}{2};\frac{1}{4}\right]+\frac{210\sqrt{3}}{105}\pi-\frac{1120}{105}=\frac{100}{9}-2\sqrt{3}\pi\end{equation} \begin{equation}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2^{n+3/2}n(n+3/2)}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1} =\frac{1}{840\sqrt{2}} \ _2F_1\left[2,2;\frac{9}{2};\frac{1}{8}\right]+\frac{11760\sqrt{7}}{840\sqrt{2}}\arcsin\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}-\frac{11200}{840\sqrt{2}} =\frac{\sqrt{14}}{9}\left(7\sqrt{7}-51\arctan\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{3^{n+3/2}n(n+3/2)}\binom{2n}{n}^{-1} =\frac{1}{2835\sqrt{3}} \ _2F_1\left[2,2;\frac{9}{2};\frac{1}{12}\right]+\frac{41580\sqrt{11}}{2835\sqrt{3}}\arcsin\frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}-\frac{40320}{2835\sqrt{3}} =\frac{4}{27\sqrt{3}}\left(73-75\sqrt{11}\arctan\frac{1}{\sqrt{11}}\right)\end{equation} From the above, we can see that; \begin{equation}_2F_1\left[2,2;\frac{9}{2};\frac{1}{4}\right] =1120-210\sqrt{3}\pi+105\left(\frac{100}{9}-2\pi\sqrt{3}\right)\approx 1.2947 _2F_1\left[2,2;\frac{9}{2};\frac{1}{8}\right] =11200-11760\sqrt{7}\arcsin\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}+\frac{560\sqrt{7}}{3}\left(7\sqrt{7}-51\arctan\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right)\approx 1.12378 _2F_1\left[2,2;\frac{9}{2};\frac{1}{12}\right] =40320-41580\sqrt{11}\arcsin\frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}+420\left(73-75\sqrt{11}\arctan\frac{1}{\sqrt{11}}\right)\approx 1.0795\end{equation} \par In light of the aforementioned conclusions, a distinct pattern is discernible.
elements.sent-2411-039: Consequently, if $k$ is a natural number, the following conjecture is proposed to hold true. \begin{equation}_2F_1\left[2,2;\frac{9}{2};\frac{1}{4k}\right]=a_k+\sqrt{4k-1}\left(b_k\arcsin\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{k}}\right)+c_k\arctan\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{4k-1}}\right)\right)\end{equation} Where the general expression of $a_k,b_k$, and $ c_k$ remains open.
